title: PM Models to Know
summary: The thinking models PMBOK 8 references, grouped by theme so you can name the family first, then pick the specific model. PMBOK 8 calls out commonly used models for leadership, motivation, conflict, change, communication, complexity, negotiation and planning - this guide catalogues them for quick recognition.
intro: The thinking models PMBOK 8 leans on - leadership, change, complexity and planning.

sections.heading: What a model is - and how PMBOK 8 uses them
sections.body:
PMBOK 8 defines a model as a thinking strategy: a simplified way to explain a process or phenomenon so you can reason about it and choose a response. You are not asked to apply every model in depth, but to recognise the right lens for a situation.

PMBOK 8 explicitly lists commonly used models by theme - situational leadership, communication, motivation, change, complexity, conflict, negotiation, planning, process groups and stakeholder salience. This guide catalogues them under those themes. Models with their own fuller guide (Tuckman, Thomas-Kilmann, motivation theories, salience) are listed at the end.

blocks.title: Exam mantra
lines: Match the model to the context; do not force one model onto every problem.
First name the family (complexity, change, leadership); then pick the specific model.
Models, methods and artifacts are chosen (tailored), not mandated.

sections.heading: Situational leadership and coaching models
sections.body: PMBOK 8 groups these as situational leadership models: match the style to the person and the task. There is no single best style.

blocks.title: Situational Leadership II - style by follower development (competence x commitment)

rows.value: Low competence, high commitment - tell and show.
Some competence, low commitment - explain and persuade.
High competence, variable commitment - encourage and share decisions.
High competence, high commitment - hand over responsibility.

blocks.title: Coaching conversation frames -> what it says

rows.value: Goal, Reality (current state), Options, Will (way forward and commitment). A single coaching conversation.
Outcome, Situation, Choices, Actions, Review. Longer-term development with built-in follow-up.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: There is no single best leadership style - the answer is the one matched to the follower's development level and the task.

sections.heading: Change models: the people side
sections.body: PMBOK 8 references change models for how people and organisations move through change - not the technical rollout. A favourite exam axis is the LEVEL each one operates at: the whole organisation/system, or one individual.

blocks.title: Organisation / system level -> what it says

rows.value: Unfreeze (loosen the status quo), change (move to the new way), refreeze (embed it). The simplest frame.
Top-led, org-wide sequence: create urgency, build coalition and vision, enable action and short-term wins, anchor change in the culture.
Late status quo -> resistance -> chaos (performance dips) -> integration -> new status quo. Tracks a GROUP's performance and normalises the temporary dip.
The adoption sequence each PERSON passes through: Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement.
Separates external change from each person's internal transition: Ending (letting go), Neutral Zone (messy in-between), New Beginning. People transition slower than systems change.
Emotional stages a person moves through: denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance. Reads resistance and grief during change.

blocks.title: Cue
lines: Level: INDIVIDUAL = ADKAR, Bridges, Kubler-Ross (one person at a time).
Level: ORGANISATION/system = Lewin, Kotter, Satir.
Process vs experience: Lewin/Kotter describe the change PROCESS; Bridges/Satir/Kubler-Ross describe the human EXPERIENCE.
People lag the system change - plan for the dip.

sections.heading: Complexity models: Cynefin and Stacey
sections.body: PMBOK 8 references complexity models to decide how much rigour and how iterative your approach should be. Cynefin sorts a situation into domains; each calls for a different response.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 304 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='12' y='12' width='140' height='88' fill='#2F6F5E' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='152' y='12' width='140' height='88' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='12' y='100' width='140' height='88' fill='#B5462F' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='152' y='100' width='140' height='88' fill='#C98A1F' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><text x='82' y='46' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Complex</text><text x='82' y='62' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>probe-sense-respond</text><text x='222' y='46' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Complicated</text><text x='222' y='62' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>sense-analyse-respond</text><text x='82' y='138' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Chaotic</text><text x='82' y='154' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>act-sense-respond</text><text x='222' y='138' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Clear</text><text x='222' y='154' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>sense-categorise-respond</text><circle cx='152' cy='100' r='26' fill='currentColor' fill-opacity='0.09' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='133' y='95.5' width='38' height='11' fill='%BG%'/><text x='152' y='103.5' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>Disorder</text></svg>
figure.caption: Cynefin: match the response to the kind of problem; Disorder is not yet knowing which domain you are in.

rows.value: Cause and effect are plain. Sense-categorise-respond; apply best practice.
Knowable but needs expertise. Sense-analyse-respond; apply good practice.
Cause and effect only clear in hindsight. Probe-sense-respond; let practice emerge via safe-to-fail experiments.
No clear cause and effect; act first. Act-sense-respond to establish order.
The centre - you do not yet know which domain you are in.

blocks.title: Stacey matrix - agreement x certainty -> what it says

rows.value: Agreement on requirements and certainty on approach - simple; suggests predictive delivery.
Complicated or complex; favours adaptive or hybrid approaches.
Low agreement and low certainty - tends toward chaos.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: Complex and adaptive go together: high uncertainty or low agreement points to iterative, experiment-led delivery.

sections.heading: Planning and team-performance models
sections.body: PMBOK 8 references planning models that capture the cost trade-off in planning effort, and team models that describe how a team matures.

rows.value: Too little planning = costly rework and missed risks; too much = wasted effort and delay. The sweet spot minimises total cost and shifts with how predictable the work is.

Seven stages a team moves through, from orientation and trust building (creating the team) to implementation, high performance and renewal (sustaining the team). Complements Tuckman by adding the resolving question at each stage.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: On highly uncertain work, plan less up front and re-plan often - the sweet spot moves left.

sections.heading: Motivation, conflict, communication and other model families
sections.body: PMBOK 8 also references motivation, conflict, communication, negotiation and salience models. These are covered in depth in their own guides; here are the family pointers so the catalogue is complete.

blocks.title: Family -> models (see the dedicated guide for depth)

rows.value: Tuckman - forming, storming, norming, performing, adjourning.
Thomas-Kilmann - competing, collaborating, compromising, avoiding, accommodating.
Maslow, Herzberg, McGregor X/Y, McClelland, Vroom and others.
Sender-receiver / communication models - channels, encoding, noise, feedback.
Salience and Power-Interest - prioritising stakeholders.
Reaching and recording a decision.

blocks.title: Exam tip
lines: In the exam, first name the family (complexity, change, leadership), then pick the specific model.
Use the dedicated guides for depth; use this one to recognise the leadership, change, complexity and planning models at a glance.

sections.heading: Models, methods, and artifacts as one toolkit
sections.body:
PMBOK frames delivery support as three complementary layers. Models are thinking lenses that help you interpret a situation. Methods are ways of doing work - techniques you run to estimate, gather data, facilitate, or decide. Artifacts are the tangible outputs - plans, baselines, logs, reports, and visual summaries that carry shared meaning.

On the exam, the right choice is rarely 'use a model' in the abstract. You pick the lens, apply a method, and update the artifact that stakeholders will use. Tailoring means choosing enough structure for the risk and uncertainty, not stacking every tool because it exists.
sections.tip: If a scenario names a document (charter, register, baseline), think artifact. If it names a technique (interview, parametric estimate, retrospective), think method. If it names a frame for reasoning (Cynefin, ADKAR, situational leadership), think model.

groups.label: What
lines: Simplified frames for leadership, change, complexity, motivation, and similar themes.

groups.label: Use
lines: Name the problem type first, then choose a response style that fits.

groups.label: What
lines: Repeatable techniques for estimating, analysis, facilitation, and decision support.

groups.label: Use
lines: Produce evidence and shared understanding you can act on.

groups.label: What
lines: Approved or living documents that capture decisions, baselines, risks, and status.

groups.label: Use
lines: Align stakeholders, measure performance, and leave an audit trail.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Models, methods, and artifacts are tailored - none are mandatory for every project. Match the tool to uncertainty, governance needs, and stakeholder demand for transparency.

sections.heading: Methods that travel across domains
sections.body:
Many methods are domain-agnostic: you reuse them for scope, schedule, cost, risk, quality, and stakeholder work. The exam cares that you pick a method that fits data quality and time pressure, not that you memorize a vendor checklist.

Three clusters appear often: data gathering and analysis, estimating, and meetings or events. Each can support planning early and control later.
sections.tip: When information is thin, prefer expert judgment, analogous estimates, and facilitated workshops. When history and metrics exist, lean on parametric models and quantitative analysis.

rows.value: Interviews, surveys, observation, document review, focus groups - collect facts and perspectives before deciding.
Root cause, alternatives, variance, trend, and earned value style thinking - turn data into options and forecasts.
Analogous (fast, history-based), parametric (formula from drivers), bottom-up (detailed), three-point (uncertainty).
Kickoff, standups, reviews, retrospectives, steering - purpose first, agenda and outcomes always documented.

items.title: Check what you know
lines: Similar past work available? Analogous is often enough for early ranges.
Clear cost or effort drivers with history? Parametric scales cleanly.

items.title: Match precision to the decision
lines: Rough order of magnitude suits go/no-go and funding envelopes.
Definitive estimates fit baseline commits when scope is stable enough.

items.title: Expose uncertainty
lines: Use ranges or three-point estimates when risk is high.
Record assumptions so later variance has a baseline explanation.

items.text: Scale from a similar past project; quick but sensitive to how similar the work really is.
Apply a rate or unit cost (for example cost per square foot or hours per feature) using historical data.
Estimate work packages then roll up; more accurate when decomposition is solid, more effort up front.
Optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic values to reflect uncertainty instead of a single false precision.

sections.heading: Baselines, logs, and registers
sections.body:
Control artifacts turn plans into measurable commitments and living memory. Baselines are approved snapshots used to measure performance. Logs and registers are working stores that track what is happening now - issues, risks, changes, decisions, and assumptions.

The Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB) typically integrates scope, schedule, and cost. You measure progress against those approved versions, not against the latest draft still in someone's notebook.
sections.tip: Changing a baseline is a governance event (change control or agile product decision, depending on approach). Updating a register entry is ordinary delivery hygiene.

groups.label: What
lines: Approved scope, schedule, and cost references (often as an integrated PMB).

groups.label: Use
lines: Compare planned vs actual, compute variance, and decide whether replanning is needed.

groups.label: What
lines: Chronological records of events such as issues, decisions, or change requests.

groups.label: Use
lines: Trace accountability, follow open items, and support audits or lessons learned.

groups.label: What
lines: Structured inventories - risk register, stakeholder register, assumption log as a living set.

groups.label: Use
lines: Prioritize responses, assign owners, and keep analysis current as the project learns.

rows.value: Approved scope statement, WBS, and WBS dictionary - the agreed product and work boundary.
Approved timeline used for start/finish and float comparisons.
Time-phased approved budget for measuring cost performance.
Current problems that need resolution now (not uncertain future events).
Identified risks, analysis, owners, and responses updated through the life cycle.
Requested and decided changes with status and disposition.

sections.heading: Plans, reports, and strategy artifacts
sections.body:
Plans describe how the team will deliver. Reports describe how delivery is going. Strategy artifacts connect the project to why the organization funded it.

The project management plan integrates subsidiary plans (scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, communications, risk, procurement, stakeholder engagement, and more). You do not invent every subsidiary plan from scratch on every project - you tailor depth to risk and governance. Reports then tell sponsors and teams whether reality still matches the plan and the business case.
sections.tip: A status report that only lists activities is weak. Exam-strong reporting answers: progress vs baseline, risks and issues, decisions needed, and forecast to complete.

rows.value: Integrated how-we-will-deliver document; may be lightweight or formal depending on approach.
Focused playbooks for scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk, communications, and similar areas.
Regular health snapshots for decisions, not vanity activity lists.
Strategy-facing justification: problem, options, value, and ongoing viability checks.
Time-oriented view of major outcomes and dependencies for leadership alignment.
Multi-perspective performance (financial, customer, process, learning) when strategy tracking matters.

items.title: Anchor why
lines: Confirm the business case, success measures, and constraints still hold.
Capture strategy intent in charter-level and roadmap-level artifacts.

items.title: Plan how
lines: Build or tailor the management plan and baselines appropriate to uncertainty.
Define how progress, risks, and changes will be reported.

items.title: Report truthfully
lines: Compare actuals to baselines and highlight decisions needed.
Escalate when value or feasibility drifts, not only when a task slips.

sections.heading: Agreements, hierarchy charts, and visual information
sections.body:
Some artifacts shape relationships and understanding more than day-to-day task tracking. Agreements define mutual expectations between parties. Contracts are legally enforceable agreements - stronger form, higher formality. Hierarchy charts show structure: work breakdown, organizational breakdown, or product breakdown. Visual data turns tables into meaning for busy stakeholders.

Use the lightest agreement that still protects both sides and matches organizational policy. Use hierarchy charts when decomposition and ownership are the confusion. Use visuals when volume of data is drowning the message.
sections.tip: MOUs and SLAs can coordinate behavior without being full commercial contracts. If money, liability, or formal remedies are central, expect contract language and change clauses.

items.text: Any mutual understanding of roles, expectations, or terms - formal or informal.
A legally enforceable agreement; often includes acceptance, change, and remedies.
Memorandum of understanding - alignment document that may not create full legal obligations.
Service level agreement - measurable service targets and response expectations.

groups.label: What
lines: Top-down breakdowns of work (WBS), organization, or product structure.

groups.label: Use
lines: Clarify scope boundaries, ownership, and how pieces roll up to outcomes.

groups.label: What
lines: Charts, dashboards, burn charts, network diagrams, and information radiators.

groups.label: Use
lines: Surface trends, bottlenecks, and KPI movement faster than raw tables.

groups.label: What
lines: Matrices (RACI, stakeholder), assumption logs, checklists, and decision records.

groups.label: Use
lines: Fill gaps that plans and baselines do not cover day to day.

sections.heading: Applying the toolkit across performance domains
sections.body:
Performance domains (stakeholders, team, development approach and life cycle, planning, project work, delivery, measurement, uncertainty, and related focus areas) all draw from the same toolkit. A risk register supports uncertainty and measurement. A communication plan supports stakeholders and team. Estimating methods feed planning and measurement. Models help you interpret which domain is under stress.

Exam questions often hide the domain inside a story. Your job is to select the model, method, or artifact that restores value with the least unnecessary process.
sections.tip: When stuck, ask: Do I need a better lens (model), a better technique (method), or a clearer shared record (artifact)? Then pick the lightest option that fits risk and governance.

rows.value: Apply a complexity or change model before locking a heavy plan.
Run targeted data gathering, expert judgment, or a facilitated workshop.
Compare to baselines, update forecasts, and surface variance in a report.
Issues go to the issue log; risks stay in the risk register with responses.
Revisit business case, roadmap, and benefits measures with sponsors.
Check agreements and contract change paths before informal promises.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Agile, lean, hybrid, and predictive approaches still use models, methods, and artifacts - they differ in cadence and formality, not in the need for shared understanding and evidence.

source: PM Cram — aligned to the current PMBOK Guide and PMI Examination Content Outline.
